facts: The Great Lakes of North America—Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie, and Ontario—were carved by glacial ice sheets during the last ice age, not by tectonic forces like Lake Baikal.
Lake Superior is the largest of the Great Lakes by surface area, while Lake Baikal in Siberia holds more freshwater by volume than all five Great Lakes combined.
The Great Lakes form the world's largest freshwater surface system, but Lake Baikal contains the world's largest volume of freshwater because it is exceptionally deep.
Glacial activity scoured out the basins of the Great Lakes, whereas Lake Baikal was formed by tectonic rifting, a process where the Earth's crust pulls apart.
misconceptions: Students think all large lakes are formed the same way, but the Great Lakes were carved by glaciers while Lake Baikal was created by tectonic rifting.
Students believe Lake Superior holds the most freshwater of any lake because it is the largest by surface area, but Lake Baikal actually contains more water by volume due to its great depth.
